real_questions: What's your operating range for capital?
Are there any capital raises assumed in your plan?
Any limits from regulators around how fast you can grow the bank?
What is the minimum amount of buffer maintained above well-capitalized?
How does targeted mortgage market share tie back to targeted balance sheet size?
What is your run-rate capital expectation over the long term, and your 18–24 month capital trajectory?

real_questions.text: Why have you — or why have you not — opted into ERBA, and what did it do to your capital ratios?
How would you handle long-term debt (LTD) requirements if they were finalized?

real_questions: Capital requirements are falling — will the released capacity be used to increase payouts or to increase risk-taking?
common_pitfalls: Issuers respond to reduced regulatory burden (Basel 3 Finalization easing, less severe CCAR stress testing for >$100B banks) by planning to lower capital levels. Moody's views this as credit negative.
strong_answer_markers: Comes prepared with an explicit 18–24 month capital view and a long-term run-rate capital target.
Answers AOCI questions in terms of the all-in management buffer (CET1 minimum + regulatory buffers + AOCI-volatility buffer + loss buffer), not just the current mark.
Frames capital deployment under falling requirements within a disciplined, stated buffer framework — not as an opportunity to run leaner.
agency_intel: Post-SVB, Moody's revised its methodology to be more punitive on capital — the same capital levels now map to lower sub-scores on the Moody's Scorecard.

Moody's capital ratio mechanics: the numerator is Tangible Common Equity (their own metric, comparable to CET1) over regulatory risk-weighted assets — the focus is on simple common-equity capital metrics.
Run-rate capital measured against Moody's framework is a large input into the rating — expect them to test the issuer's run-rate capital directly against their scorecard.

Moody's is more concerned than S&P about capital rule uncertainty and the prospect of lower capital requirements. Expect stricter questions on how the issuer plans to keep capital levels higher in the face of reduced regulatory burden.

For CCAR banks (>$100B assets), Moody's specifically watches the gap between the now-lower regulatory minimum and the operating capital level. A bank that lets the buffer compress simply because rules allow it will be flagged.

Moody's treats AOCI as a secondary metric that everyone converges on once it's formally in regulatory requirements. The probe is less about the raw number and more about how AOCI volatility is built into capital-management targets.

For UK banks: the Bank of England announced (Dec 2025) that the Tier 1 capital benchmark moves from 14% to 13%, driven by lower average risk weights, revised risk measurement, and reduced systemic importance of some banks since 2015. Agencies read this simplification and recalibration as broadly negative for UK bank ratings — expect questions on how the lower benchmark changes capital targets and distribution plans.

For Swiss banks: Switzerland is the exception to the global deregulatory trend — the too-big-to-fail framework would increase capital requirements. The Swiss conversation is about absorbing higher requirements; the UK and North American conversation is about how much of the released capital gets paid out.

For EU banks: whether the EU Commission's bank-competitiveness report supports keeping capital requirements stable or proposes reducing them is an open question. A reduction proposal would accelerate a credit-negative trend of gradually falling requirements — the agencies are actively forming their view, and management's stated intentions feed directly into that assessment.

For Canadian D-SIBs: OSFI lowered the domestic stability buffer and the top of its range (Jun 2026), giving banks more flexibility to deploy capital. Moody's views this as credit negative — it facilitates lower capital at banks already running thinner buffers above minimums. The predictable question: does the lower buffer mean you will deploy capital or run with lower capital over time, and how should the added risk-taking that comes with lower capital be thought about?
